summary: Gruber responds to criticism that his stance on tracking has shifted from opposing Meta's tracking to defending it. He clarifies that he has always opposed non-consensual tracking specifically, not tracking itself, which is why he supports Apple's App Tracking Transparency — it requires consent rather than banning tracking outright. He argues Meta's 'Pay or OK' model in the EU gives users a clear, fair choice, and that the EU's opposition to it is paternalistic. He contends that the EU's proper role is ensuring informed consent and user control over data, not deciding on behalf of citizens that all tracking is inherently wrong.
key_points: Gruber's position has been consistent: non-consensual and surreptitious tracking is wrong, but consensual tracking with informed users is acceptable
Apple's ATT succeeded precisely because it requires consent rather than banning tracking — the 'Allow' option is the point, not a flaw
Meta's 'Pay or OK' model offers the exact kind of clear choice that Margrethe Vestager herself advocated for in 2018, yet the EU now opposes the results
The EU's stance has shifted from requiring informed consent to opposing all tracking regardless of consent, which Gruber calls paternalistic and infantilizing
Most EU users given a clear choice prefer free services with targeted ads over paying for ad-free experiences
money_quotes: It's non-consensual tracking I object to, especially tracking that's downright surreptitious. Not tracking in and of itself.
Tracking is wrong when it's done without consent, and when users have no idea what's being tracked or how it's being used. Tracking is fine when it's done with consent.
Privacy doesn't mean never being tracked. It means never being tracked without clear consent.
It's paternalistic — infantilizing even — to believe that government bureaucrats should take these decisions out of the hands of EU citizens.
The privacy zealots leading this crusade in the EU do not think people are smart, and do not think they should be trusted to make these decisions for themselves.
It turns out that when given the clear choice, the overwhelming majority of EU denizens prefer to use Meta's platforms free-of-charge with targeted ads.
The government's proper role is to ensure people can make an informed choice, and that they have control over their own data. That's what I thought four years ago, and it's what I think now.
key_insight: Privacy is about informed consent and user control, not about banning tracking entirely — and governments that override individual choice in the name of privacy are being paternalistic, not protective.
